research.title: Type-Safe Server Actions Showdown: next-safe-action vs zsa vs Rolling Your Own
research.whyTrending: Two competing libraries (next-safe-action, zsa) formalize validation + middleware for server actions; a comparison with a hand-rolled baseline has clear SEO potential.

seo.suggestedTitle: next-safe-action vs zsa vs Rolling Your Own

gaps: Comparisons skip the DIY baseline that shows WHAT the libraries actually replace
Nobody shows the same action three ways (raw, zsa, next-safe-action) side by side
Migration between the two / from DIY not covered
When rolling your own is the right call (few actions) rarely admitted
strengths: zsa DEV guide has clean procedure examples

officialSources.note: v7: chained .use() middleware, metadata, useAction/useOptimisticAction hooks, Standard Schema
maturity: next-safe-action ~3.5k stars more mature, zsa newer/smaller cleaner API

expertInsights: Both wrap server actions with validation, typed errors, middleware-like composition
next-safe-action: chainable .use() middleware, typed ctx propagation, client hooks useAction/useOptimisticAction, metadata
zsa: procedural createServerAction factory; createServerActionProcedure for authed procedures chained to actions
next-safe-action more mature + bigger ecosystem; zsa less boilerplate for basic actions
zsa pairs nicely if TanStack Query already in stack
Both use zod / Standard Schema for input validation
commonProblems: Cargo-culting a library without understanding the DIY middleware it replaces
Rolling your own inconsistently across many actions
Mixing both libraries in one codebase

strategy.angle: Show the SAME action three ways — hand-rolled wrapper, zsa, next-safe-action — so the reader sees exactly what each library buys and can make an informed choice instead of following a boilerplate. Give an honest DIY-is-fine threshold and a clear default recommendation
strategy.title: Type-Safe Server Actions: next-safe-action vs zsa vs DIY
strategy.excerpt: next-safe-action and zsa both turn server actions into validated, typed, middleware-wrapped functions. The fastest way to choose is to see the same action built three ways — including by hand — so you know exactly what youre buying before you add a dependency.
hooks: Contrarian: before you add either library, write the DIY version once — it is 15 lines and it teaches you what youre paying for
Practical: the same deleteTask action, three ways, side by side
Honest: with under ~5 actions, rolling your own is genuinely the right call
outline: Hook: the wrapper you keep copy-pasting
The baseline: rolling your own in 15 lines
Side-by-side: the same deleteTask three ways
Client hooks: useAction vs the DIY story
